# Overview of Supervised Learning

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(reticulate)
library(bookdown)
library(MASS)
library(mvtnorm)
library(ggplot2)
library(class)

# Use bookdown::serve_book()
set.seed(20021122)
use_python("C:\\Python314\\python.exe") # Set to your local python path

color <- function(x, color) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    sprintf("\\textcolor{%s}{%s}", color, x)
  } else if (knitr::is_html_output()) {
    sprintf("<span style='color: %s;'>%s</span>", color,
            x)
  } else {
    x
  }
}
```

## Variable Types and Terminologies

Symbols and variable conventions follow the rules outlined below by the textbook: $\newline$ Input variable is denoted by the symbol $X$. If $X$ is a vector, then its components is accessed by subscripts $X_j$. Quantitative outputs are denoted by $Y$, and qualitative outputs by $G$. Observed values are written in lowercase; for example the $i$th observed value of $X$ is written as $x_i$ (where $x_i$ is a scalar or vector). Matrices are represented by bold uppercase letters; for example, a set of $N$ input $p$-vectors $x_i$, $i = 1, ... , N$ is represented by the $N \times p$ matrix $\textbf{X}$. In general, vectors will not be bold, except when they have $N$ components; this convention distinguishes a $p$-vector of inputs $x_i$ for the $i$th observations from the $N$-vector $\textbf{x}_j$ consisting of all the observations on variable $X_j$. Since all vectors are assumed to be column vectors, the $i$th row of $\textbf{X}$ is $x_i^{T}$, the vector transpose of $x_i$.

For most learning tasks, we can understand it as such: given an input vector $X$, we want to make a good prediction of the output $Y$, denoted as $\hat{Y}$. If $Y\in\mathbb{R}$ then $\hat{Y}$ too; likewise for categorical outputs, $\hat{G}$ should take values in the same set $\mathcal{G}$ associated with $G$.

For accurate prediction, data is necessary. We denote training data as a set of measurements $(x_i,y_i)$ or $(x_i,g_i)$, $i = 1,...,N$.

## Prediction Approaches: Least Squares and Nearest Neighbors

Two prediction methods are explored here: The linear model fit by least squares and the $k$-nearest neighbor prediction rule.

### Linear Models and Least Squares

Given a vector of inputs $X^{T} = (X_1, X_2, ..., X_n)$, we predict the output $Y$ via the model:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta_0} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_j\hat{\beta}_j
(\#eq:leq)
\end{equation}
$$

where $\hat{\beta_0}$ is the intercept. Equivalently, we can write the linear model in vector form as an inner product:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{Y} = X^{T}\hat{\beta}
(\#eq:leqvec)
\end{equation}
$$

where $X = \begin{pmatrix}1\\x_1\\\vdots\\x_n\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbb{R^{n+1}}$ and $\hat{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix}\hat{\beta}_0\\\hat{\beta}_1\\\vdots\\\hat{\beta}_n\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$, such that $\hat{Y} = \sum_{i=0}^{n}\hat{\beta}_i x_i$.

If $\textbf{X}$ is instead a feature matrix with $p$ features and $m$ samples such that $\textbf{X}\in\mathbb{R^{m\times p}}$, where $p=n+1$, we can represent $\textbf{X}^{T}$ as a $p$-dimensional row vector $\begin{bmatrix} X_0 \; X_1 \; X_2 \; ... \; X_n \end{bmatrix}$, where each $X_j$ is a column vector in $\mathbb{R^m}$. For example, $X_1^T = [x_1^{(1)} \; x_1^{(2)} \; \ldots \; x_1^{(m)}]$, $X_0$ is a constant vector of $1$ and suppose that $\hat{Y}$ is a $k$-dimensional row vector instead of a scalar, then by equation \@ref(eq:leq), $\beta$ must necessarily be a $p \times K$ matrix of coefficients.

<details>
<summary>Remark on linear model as a hyperplane.</summary>
:::{.remark}
In the $(p+1)$-dimensional input-output space, the tuple $(X, \hat{Y})$ represents a hyperplane. This follows directly from the linear structure of equation \@ref(eq:leqvec):

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\hat{Y} &= X^{T}\beta \\
        &= \sum_{i=0}^{n} \beta_i x_i \\
        &= \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n \beta_i x_i
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Swapping the LHS with the RHS term:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
-\beta_0 &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i x_i - \hat{Y}\\
         &= \sum_{i=1}^n \beta_i x_i + (-1)\hat{Y}\\
         &= (\beta_1 \; \beta_2 \; ... \beta_n)\;\cdot\;(x_1 \; x_2 \; ... \; \hat{Y})^T \\
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

A hyperplane is defined as $\textbf{w}^{T}\textbf{z}= b$. In our case, by letting $\textbf{w} = (\beta_1 \; \beta_2 \; ... \beta_n)^T$ and $\textbf{z}=(x_1 \; x_2 \;...\;\hat{Y})^T$, we obtain $\textbf{w}^{T}\textbf{z} = -\beta_0$ (our intercept $\beta_0$ translates the hyperplane away from the origin).
:::
</details>

### Residual Sum of Squares

To fit our training data to the linear model, we use the method of least squares. We pick the coefficients $\beta$ that minimize the residual sum of square:

$$
\begin{equation}
RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - x_{i}^{T}\beta)^2
(\#eq:RSS)
\end{equation}
$$

$RSS(\beta)$ is a quadratic function of the parameters, hence its minimum always exist but may not be unique.

<details>
<summary>Remark on the existence and non-uniqueness of a minimum for RSS</summary>
:::{.remark}
We first prove that a minimum always exists for the RSS by establishing that RSS is a convex function with at least one critical point, which guarantees a global minimum.

To analyze the structure of RSS, we expand the quadratic form:

$$
\begin{equation}
RSS(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - x_{i}^{T}\beta)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i^2 + (x_{i}^{T}\beta)^2 - 2y_ix_i^{T}\beta)
\end{equation}
$$

By linearity of summation and the dot product identities $\sum_{i=1}^N x_ix_i^T= \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ and $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^N x_iy_i$, this becomes:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
RSS(\beta) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N}\beta^Tx_ix_i^T\beta - \sum_{i=1}^{N}2y_ix_i^{T}\beta\\
           &= ||\textbf{y}||^2 +\beta^T\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta - 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}\beta
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

To establish convexity for RSS, we show its Hessian matrix $\nabla^2 RSS(\beta) = 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is positive semidefinite as RSS is twice differentiable. A matrix M is positive semidefinite if $x^TMx \geq 0$ for all $x$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. As:

$$
\begin{equation}
v^T\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}v = (\textbf{X}v)^T\textbf{X}v = ||\textbf{X}v||^2\geq0
\end{equation}
$$

, we showed that RSS is convex.

Next, we need to establish that a critical point exists and this critical point must necessarily be a minimum. To find a critical point, we compute the gradient[^1]:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\nabla_{\beta}RSS(\beta) &= -2\textbf{X}^T(\textbf{y}-\textbf{X}\beta) \\
                         &= -2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} + 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Thus, a critical point must satisfy the normal equation $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta$. The problem of determining the existence of a critical point can now be reduced to a simple problem of determining that the normal equation is consistent:

A linear system $Ax = b$ has a solution if and only if $b \in \text{Col}(A)$. For the normal equations $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}$, we need $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \in \text{col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$. Since $\text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}) = \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T)$[^2] and $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}$ is, by definition, a linear combination of the columns of $\textbf{X}^T$, we have $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \in \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T) = \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$. Since a consistent system has at least one solution, RSS always has at least one critical point.

Lastly, we show that for convex functions, any critical point is necessarily a global minimum. Let $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ be convex and differentiable at $x^*$ for any $x\in\mathbb{R}^n$, and $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$, then $x^*$ is a global minimum. By the first-order characterization of convexity[^3]:

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) \geq f(x^*) + \nabla f^T(x^*)(x-x^*) =  f(x^*) + 0(x-x^*) = f(x^*)
\end{equation}
$$

As $f(x) \geq f(x^*)$ for any $x,x^*\in dom(f)$, $f(x^*)$ is a global minimum. Therefore a minimum always exists for RSS.

However, this minimum is not necessarily unique. The uniqueness depends entirely on whether $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible, leading to two cases:

Case 1: $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible:

If the matrix $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible, then:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat\beta = (\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})^{-1}\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}
\end{equation}
$$

, and $\hat{\beta}$ is a unique solution.

Case 2: $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is singular:

When $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is singular, $\text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$ is nontrivial. If $\beta_0$ is a solution to the normal equation and $\beta^*\in\text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$, such that $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta^* = 0$, then:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}(\beta_0 + \beta^*) = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta_0 + \textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta^* = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} + 0 = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Hence $\beta_0 + \beta^*$ is itself a solution and it forms the solution set $\{\beta_0 + \beta^*|\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta = \textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \wedge \beta^*\in \text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})\}$. Since $\text{Ker}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})$ is a nontrivial linear subspace and has infinitely many elements[^4], the solution set forms an affine subspace with infinitely many elements.

Hence, while the minimum always exists, it is only unique when $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is invertible.
:::
</details>
<br>
As shown previously, $\text{RSS}(\beta)$ can be written in the matrix notation:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{RSS}(\beta) &= \textbf{y}^T\textbf{y} +\beta^T\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}\beta - 2\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}\beta\\
                  &= (\textbf{y}-\textbf{X}^T\beta)^T(y-\textbf{X}^T\beta)
\end{split}
(\#eq:RSSmatrix)
\end{equation}
$$

where $\textbf{X}$ is an $N \times p$ matrix with each row an input vector, ,and $\textbf{y}$ is an $N$-vector of the outputs in the training set. Differentiating w.r.t $\beta$ we get the normal equations:

$$
\begin{equation}
\textbf{X}^T(\textbf{y} - \textbf{X}\beta) = 0
(\#eq:normeq)
\end{equation}
$$
If $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ is nonsingular, then we get the unique solution:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat\beta = (\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})^{-1}\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y}
\end{equation}
(\#eq:normsol)
$$

and the fitted value at the $i$th input $x_i$ is $\hat{y}_i = \hat{y}_i(x_i) = x^{T}_i\hat{\beta}$. At an arbitrary input $x_0$, the prediction is $\hat{y}_i(x_0) = x^{T}_0\hat{\beta}$. The entire fitted surface is characterized by the $p$ parameters $\hat{\beta}$.[^5] It seems that we may not need a very large data set to fit such a model.

### Linear Model Simulation

We simulate a data set with an output class variable $G$ with the values `r color("BLUE", "blue")` or `r color("ORANGE", "orange")`. There are 100 points in each of the two classes. We fit a linear regression model to these values, with the response $Y$ coded as $0$ for `r color("BLUE", "blue")` and $1$ for `r color("ORANGE", "orange")`. The fitted values $\hat{Y}$ are converted to a fitted class variable $\hat{G}$ according to the rule

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{G} =
\begin{cases}
\text{ORANGE} & \hat{Y} > 0.5\\
\text{BLUE} & \hat{Y} \leq 0.5
\end{cases}
(\#eq:classlabel)
\end{equation}
$$

There are multiple scenarios where our constructed data can come from, but we outline here specifically two types:

$\textbf{Scenario 1}$: The training data in each class were generated from bivariate Gaussian distributions with uncorrelated components and different means.

In other words, our data is generated from each class $j$ with fixed mean $\mu^{(j)}$ and covariance $\Sigma^{(j)}$, and each observation $i$ is distributed with $X_i|Y=j \sim N(\mu^{(j)}, \Sigma^{(j)})$. Therefore, $P(X|Y=j) = \phi(x;\mu^{(j)},\Sigma^{(j)})$.

$\textbf{Scenario 2}$: The training data in each class came from a mixture of 10 low-variance Gaussian distributions, with individual means themselves distributed as Gaussian.

Essentially, we have $10$ random cluster locations $\mu_k^{(j)}$ per class, each distributed around a class specific mean $\mu^{(j)}$ with covariance $\Sigma^{(j)}$. Then for each cluster location, we generate an observation $X_i$ where $X_i|\text{Location}=k,Y=j \sim N(\mu_k^{(j)}, \Sigma)$ where $\Sigma$ is the variance of the mixture distribution. Therefore, $P(X|Y=j) = \sum_{k=1}^{10}\pi_k\phi(x;\mu_k^{(j)},\Sigma)$, where $\pi_k$ is the weight of the cluster (How likely/prominent this cluster is).

We first demonstrate the method of how our simulated data set is generated:

```{r, warning=FALSE}
# Generate data set of n=20 from two bivariate normal distribution
d1 <- mvrnorm(n = 10, c(1, 0), diag(2)) #BLUE
d2 <- mvrnorm(n = 10, c(0, 1), diag(2)) #ORANGE

gen_obs <- function(mean, nobs) {
  obs <- c()
  for (i in 1:nobs){
    row_mean <- mean[sample(1:10, 1), ]
    newobs <- mvrnorm(n = 1, row_mean, diag(1 / 5, 2, 2))
    obs <- rbind(obs, unname(newobs))
  }

  colnames(obs) <- c("x1", "x2")
  obs
}

d1_obs <- gen_obs(d1, 100)
d2_obs <- gen_obs(d2, 100)
Y <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 100) # 0=BLUE, 1=ORANGE
test_mat <- as.data.frame(cbind(Y, rbind(d1_obs, d2_obs)))
test_mat$Y <- factor(test_mat$Y, labels = c("Blue", "Orange"))

# Generate points grid
grid_size <- 100
x1_range <- range(test_mat$x1)
x2_range <- range(test_mat$x2)
grid_x1 <- seq(x1_range[1] - 1, x1_range[2] + 1, length.out = grid_size)
grid_x2 <- seq(x2_range[1] - 1, x2_range[2] + 1, length.out = grid_size)
grid_data <- expand.grid(x1 = grid_x1, x2 = grid_x2)
```

We generated $10$ centers $m_k^{(1)} \sim N((0,1)^T, I)$ for `r color("ORANGE", "orange")` class and $10$ centers $m_k^{(0)} \sim N((1,0)^T, I)$ for `r color("BLUE", "blue")` class. Then, to create a Gaussian mixture model, we select a mean $m_k$ from each class with probability $\pi_k = \frac{1}{10}$ and generate an observation from $N(m_k, I/5)$. We generate $100$ observations for each class using this method and train our model on the data.

```{r linearmodel, fig.cap="Classification example in two dimensions.", max_height = "50px", warning=FALSE}
# Fit simple linear model
lm_data <- data.frame(Y = as.numeric(test_mat$Y == "Orange"), x1 = test_mat$x1, x2 = test_mat$x2)
boundary_model <- lm(Y ~ x1 + x2, data = lm_data)

grid_predictions <- predict(boundary_model, grid_data)
grid_data$predicted_class <- ifelse(grid_predictions > 0.5, "Orange", "Blue")

# Create the plot with background
p <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class),
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y),
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  labs(title = "Linear Regression") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)

# Add decision boundary line
boundary_slope <- -coef(boundary_model)[2] / coef(boundary_model)[3]
boundary_intercept <- (0.5 - coef(boundary_model)[1]) / coef(boundary_model)[3]

p + geom_abline(slope = boundary_slope, intercept = boundary_intercept,
                color = "black", linewidth = 1)
```

In \@ref(fig:linearmodel), the set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ classified as `r color("ORANGE", "orange")` corresponds to $\{x:x^T\hat{\beta}\gt0.5\}$, and the two predicted classes are separated by the linear $\textit{decision boundary}\;\{x:x^T\hat{\beta} = 0.5\}$. There are some misclassifications in the simulated data.

### Nearest-Neighbor Methods

The nearest-neighborhood methods use observations in the training set $\mathcal{T}: \{(x_i, y_i),\;i=1,2,...,N\}$ closest in input space to $x$ to obtain $\hat{Y}$. We define the $k$-nearest neighbor fit for $\hat{Y}$ as

$$
\hat{Y}(x) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{x_i\in N_k(x)} y_i
$$

, where $N_k(x) = \{x_i \in \mathcal{T}:d(x_i,x) \leq d_{(k)}(x)\}$ is the $k$-nearest neighborhood of point $x$ and $|N_k| = k$. Here $d_{(k)}(x)$ denotes the $k$-th smallest distance from $x$ to the training points,  and $d(x_i,x)$ is the Euclidean norm $||x_i-x||_2$.[^6] The fitted values $\hat{Y}$ is converted to a class label $\hat{G}$ as in Equation \@ref(eq:classlabel).

```{r KNNexample, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", fig.cap="Example of a 3-NN algorithm."}
knitr::include_graphics("Chp2/pictures/note2_1.png")
```

We can further define $k$-NN more formally as:

Given test point $\textbf{x}$, we define the set of the $k$ nearest neighbors of the neighborhood $N_k$. $N_k$ is formally defined as $N_k \subseteq \mathcal{T}$ s.t. $|N_k| = k$. Furthermore, given $\forall(\textbf{x}', y') \in \mathcal{T}\backslash N_k$,[^7]

$$
d(\textbf{x}, \textbf{x}') \geq \operatorname*{argmax}_{(\textbf{x}'', y'') \in N_k} d(\textbf{x}, \textbf{x}'') 
$$

We can now define a classifier $h$ as a function returning the most common label in $N_k$:

$$
h(\textbf{x}) = \text{mode}(\{y'': (x', y') \in N_k\})
$$
, where we classify the neighborhood by the label of the highest occurrence.

```{r knn1, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="The classes are fit by 15-nearest neighbor averaging. The predicted class is chosen by majority vote amongst the 15-nearest neighbors."}
train_features <- test_mat[, c("x1", "x2")]
test_data <- grid_data[, c("x1", "x2")]
train_labels <- test_mat$Y

# knn implementation
knn_model <- knn(train = train_features, test = test_data, cl = train_labels, k = 15)
grid_data$predicted_class <- knn_model
grid_data$pred_numeric <- as.numeric(knn_model == "Orange")

# Create the plot with background
ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class),
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_contour(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, z = pred_numeric),
               breaks = 0.5, size = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y),
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  labs(title = "15-Nearest Neighbor Classifier") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)
```

In Figure \@ref(fig:knn1), the same training data is used as in Figure \@ref(fig:linearmodel), and we used 15-nearest-neighbor averaging of the binary coded response as the method of fitting. $\hat{Y}$ is the proportion of `r color("ORANGE", "orange")`'s in the neighborhood, and so assigning class `r color("ORANGE", "orange")` to $\hat{G}$ if $\hat{Y} \gt 0.5$ is essentially a majority vote in the neighborhood. We see that the decision boundaries are irregular, non-linear, and respond to local clusters where one class dominates.

```{r knn2, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Classes are coded as a binary variable and then predicted by 1-nearest neighbor classification."}
# knn implementation
knn_model <- knn(train = train_features, test = grid_data[, c("x1", "x2")], cl = train_labels, k = 1)
grid_data$predicted_class <- knn_model
grid_data$pred_numeric <- as.numeric(knn_model == "Orange")

# Create the plot with background
ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class),
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_contour(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, z = pred_numeric),
               breaks = 0.5, size = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y),
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  labs(title = "1-Nearest Neighbor Classifier") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)
```

Figure \@ref(fig:knn2) instead shows the result for 1-nearest neighbor classification: $\hat{Y}$ is assigned the value $y_{\ell}$ of the closest point $x_{\ell}$ to $x$ in the training data. This corresponds to a Voronoi tessellation of the training data. Each point $x_i$ has an associated Voroni cell bounding the region where it is the only closest neighbor to any test point, such that, for all points $x$ in the cell, $\hat{G}(x) = g_i$. Compared to Figure \@ref(fig:knn1), the decision boundary is even more irregular. The training error is approximately an increasing function of $k$, and is always $0$ for $k=1$. As a consequence, we cannot use sum-of-squared errors on the training set as a criterion for picking $k$, as this would always result in picking $k=1$.

It appears that $k$-nearest neighbor fits have a single parameter, which is the number of neighbors $k$, compared to the $p$ parameters in least-squares fit. However, the effective number of parameters of $k$-nn is $N/k$ and is generally bigger than $p$, which decreases with increasing $k$. Consider the hypothetical case where the neighborhoods are non-overlapping, given $N$ training points and $k$ points per neighborhood, we will have $N/k$ neighborhoods. As a majority vote will be casted for each neighborhood, this effectively constitutes a parameter for each neighborhood.

```{r misclassification, warning=FALSE, fig.cap = "Misclassification curves. A single training sample of size 200 was used, and a test sample of size 10000. Orange curve is test and blue curve is training error for k-nn classification. Results for the linear regression are the orange and blue squares at 3 degree of freedom. "}
# Training set
train_obs1 <- gen_obs(d1, 5000)
train_obs2 <- gen_obs(d2, 5000)
Y <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 5000) # 0=BLUE, 1=ORANGE
train_mat <- as.data.frame(cbind(Y, rbind(train_obs1, train_obs2)))
train_mat$Y <- factor(train_mat$Y, labels = c("Blue", "Orange"))

# Test set
test_obs1 <- gen_obs(d1, 100)
test_obs2 <- gen_obs(d2, 100)
Y <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 100)
test_mat <- as.data.frame(cbind(Y, rbind(test_obs1, test_obs2)))
test_mat$Y <- factor(test_mat$Y, labels = c("Blue", "Orange"))

# KNN model
k <- c(1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 17, 21, 25, 31, 39, 45, 55, 69, 89, 101, 125, 151)
results <- data.frame(k = numeric(), test_error = numeric(), train_error = numeric())

for (kval in k){
  test_pred <- knn(train = train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], test = test_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], cl = train_mat$Y, k = kval)
  train_pred <- knn(train = train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], test = train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")], cl = train_mat$Y, k = kval)

  test_error <- mean(test_pred != test_mat$Y)
  train_error <- mean(train_pred != train_mat$Y)

  results <- rbind(results, data.frame(k = kval, test_error = test_error, train_error = train_error))
}

results$df <- 200 / results$k

# Linear
lm_data <- data.frame(Y = as.numeric(train_mat$Y == "Orange"), x1 = train_mat$x1, x2 = train_mat$x2)
lm_train <- lm(Y ~ x1 + x2, data = lm_data)

test_pred <- ifelse(predict(lm_train, test_mat[, c("x1", "x2")]) > 0.5, "Orange", "Blue")
train_pred <- ifelse(predict(lm_train, train_mat[, c("x1", "x2")]) > 0.5, "Orange", "Blue")

lm_test_error <- mean(test_pred != test_mat$Y)
lm_train_error <- mean(train_pred != train_mat$Y)
# bayes_error <- 0.21

ggplot(results, aes(x = df)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = train_error, color = "Train"), linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(aes(y = train_error, color = "Train"), size = 2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = test_error, color = "Test"), linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "solid") +
  geom_point(aes(y = test_error, color = "Test"), size = 2) +
  # geom_hline(aes(yintercept = bayes_error, color = "Bayes"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 3, y = lm_train_error, color = "Train"), shape = 15, size = 2.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x = 3, y = lm_test_error, color = "Test"), shape = 15, size = 2.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.5, y = lm_train_error + 0.01, label = "Linear", hjust = 0, size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Train" = "#4A90E2", "Test" = "#F5A623", "Bayes" = "#8B4789"),
                     breaks = c("Train", "Test", "Bayes")) +
  scale_x_log10(
    name = "Degrees of Freedom = N/k",
    breaks = c(2, 3, 5, 8, 12, 18, 29, 67, 200),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ 200 / ., name = "k = Number of Nearest Neighbors",
                        breaks = c(1, 3, 5, 7, 11, 21, 31, 45, 69, 101, 151))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Test Error", limits = c(0, 0.35), breaks = seq(0.05, 0.3, 0.05)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.15, 0.15),
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.title.x.top = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10)),
    axis.title.x.bottom = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.ticks = element_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.ticks.length = unit(0.2, "cm"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  )

```

## Statistical Decision Theory

We now develop a framework to develop and evaluate models that were previously discussed. Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^p$ denote a real valued input vector, and $Y \in \mathbb{R}$ a real-valued random output variable, with joint distribution $Pr(X,Y)$. We seek a function $f(X)$ for predicting $Y$ given input $X$. As we desire models that are accurate, we need a loss function $L(Y, f(X))$ that penalizes errors in prediction. The most common loss function is the squared-error loss: $L(Y, f(X)) = (Y - f(X))^2$. This gives us a criterion to select $f$

### Regression Function

Given the squared error loss function, we now define the expected prediction error criterion:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE}(f) &= \text{E}[(Y-f(X))^2] \\
       &= \int (y-f(x))^2\text{Pr}(dx,dy)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

By conditioning on $X$, we can write $\text{EPE}$ as:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE}(f) &= \int_{y\in Y}\int_{x\in X} (y-f(x))^2\;\text{Pr}(X=x, Y=y)\;dx\;dy \\
              &= \int_{y\in Y}\int_{x\in X} (y-f(x))^2\;\text{Pr}(Y=y|\;X=x)\;\text{Pr}(X=x)\;dx\;dy \\
              &= \int_{x\in X}\int_{y\in Y} ((y-f(x))^2\;\text{Pr}(Y=y|\;X=x)\;dy)\ \;\text{Pr}(X=x)\;dx \\
              &= \text{E}_X\text{E}_{Y|X}[(Y - f(X))^2 | X]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

We note that $\text{EPE}$ is an integral of the form:

$$
\begin{equation}
EPE(f) = \int_{x\in X} E[(Y-f(x))^2|x]\; dx
\end{equation}
$$

Given that the integral is always non-negative and $f(x)$ can be chosen independently for each $x$, minimizing each integrand is equivalent to minimize the integral. Therefore, we solve the simpler problem instead: At each point $x$, we seek to find a constant $c^*$ that minimize $E_{Y|X}[(Y-c)^2|X=x]$. In other words, it suffices to minimize $\text{EPE}$ pointwise:

<details>
<summary>Remark on justification of minimization.</summary>
::: {.remark}
Given a measure space $(S, \mathcal{F}, \mu)$, let $\mathcal{F}$ be a space of measurable functions $f: S \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ and let $F: \mathcal{F} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a functional of the form:
$$
\begin{equation}
F(f) = \int_{S} g(f(s),s) \, \mu(ds)
\end{equation}
$$
for some measurable $g: \mathcal{X} \times S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ and positive measure $\mu$ on $S$

A global minimization problem is
$$
\begin{equation}
f^* = \operatorname*{argmin}_{f\in\mathcal{F}} F(f)
\end{equation}
$$

The functional $F$ admits pointwise minimization if the global problem reduces to the family of scalar problems
$$
\begin{equation}
f^*(s) = \operatorname*{argmin}_{c\in\mathcal{X}} g(c,s)
\end{equation}
$$

For any $f$, $g(f(s), s) \geq g(f^*(s), s)$ by definition of $f^*$. Since $\mu$ is a positive measure, integration preserves inequality:

$$
\begin{equation}
F(f) = \int_{S} g(f(s),s) \, \mu(ds) \geq \int_{S} g(f^*(s),s) \, \mu(ds) = F(f^*)
\end{equation}
$$

Therefore, $f^*$ is a global minimizer for all $f \in \mathcal{F}$.  

This reduction is only valid if:

1. The integrand at $s$ depends on $f$ only through $f(s)$
2. $\mu$ is a positive measure, to guarantee the preservation of inequalities
3. The pointwise minimizer $\operatorname*{argmin}_{c} g(c,s)$ is itself measurable, hence $f^* \in \mathcal{F}$

:::
</details>
$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) = \operatorname*{argmin}_c \text{E}_{Y|X}[(Y-c)^2\mid X=x]
\end{equation}
$$

To solve this optimization problem, we first expand the inner term:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
f(x) &= \operatorname*{argmin}_c \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y^2 - 2Yc + c^2\mid X=x] \\
     &= \operatorname*{argmin}_c \bigl(\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y^2\mid X=x] - 2c\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y\mid X=x] + c^2\bigr) \\
     &= \operatorname*{argmin}_c \bigl(c^2 - 2c\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y\mid X=x] + \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y^2\mid X=x]\bigr)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

We take the derivative of the RHS with respect to $c$ and set it to $0$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
2c - 2\text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x] &= 0 \\
c = \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Hence, the solution to the optimization problem is the conditional expectation (regression function):

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) = \text{E}_{Y|X}[Y|X=x]
\end{equation}
$$

### Optimal Function of Nearest-Neighbor Method

For a fixed query point $x$, the k-NN estimator replaces the population objective $\text{E}[(Y-c)^2 \mid X = x]$ with its empirical analog over the neighborhood $N_k(x)$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\hat{c} = \text{argmin}_c \sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} (y_i - c)^2
\end{equation}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $c$ and setting to zero,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\frac{d}{dc}\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)}(y_i - c)^2 &= -2\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)}(y_i - c) = 0 \\
\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} y_i &= \sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} c \\
\hat{c} &= \frac{1}{k}\sum_{x_i \in N_k(x)} y_i = \text{Ave}(y_i \mid x_i \in N_k(x))
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

The population conditional expectation $\text{E}(Y \mid X = x)$ is approximated in two steps: the sample average estimates $\text{E}(Y \mid X \in N_k(x))$ by the law of large numbers as $k \to \infty$, and $\text{E}(Y \mid X \in N_k(x)) \to \text{E}(Y \mid X = x)$ as the neighborhood shrinks when $k/N \to 0$.

### Optimal Function of Linear Model

Assuming that the regression function $f(x)$ is approximately linear:

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) \approx x^{T}\beta
\end{equation}
$$
We plug this linear model into $\text{EPE}$,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE}(f) &= \text{E}[(Y-f(X))^2] \\
              &= \text{E}[(Y-x^T\beta)^2] \\
              &= \text{E}[Y^2 + (x^T\beta)^2 -2x^TY\beta]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

We now differentiate in terms of $\beta$ (See the appendix for this chapter for the justification of the interchangeability of the derivative and integral sign),

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\frac{\partial\text{EPE}(f)}{\partial \beta} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\int (Y^2 + (x^T\beta)^2 -2x^TY\beta)\;\text{Pr}(dx,dy) \\
                                             &= \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}(Y^2 + \beta^Txx^T\beta - 2x^TY\beta)\;\text{Pr}(dx,dy)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

By linearity of the integrals and derivatives,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\frac{\partial\text{EPE}(f)}{\partial \beta} &= \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}Y^2 + \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\beta^Txx^T\beta - \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}(2x^TY\beta) \\
                                             &= 0 + \int2xx^T\beta - \int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}2Yx^T\beta \quad (\text{Using the identity:}\;\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\beta^Txx^T\beta = 2xx^T\beta)\\
                                             &= 2\beta\int xx^T - 2\int\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta} Y\beta^Tx \quad \text{(Dot product is commutative)} \\
                                             &= 2\beta\text{E}[XX^T] - 2\text{E}[YX] \quad (\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}\beta^Tx = \frac{\partial}{\partial\beta_i}\sum\beta_ix_i = \sum x_i)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Setting $\frac{\partial\text{EPE}(f)}{\partial \beta} = 0$ to find the optimal value,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
2\beta\text{E}[XX^T] &= 2\text{E}[XY] \\
\beta &= (\text{E}[XX^T])^{-1}\text{E[XY]}
\end{split}
\end{equation}
(\#eq:linearoptimal)
$$

We have that the least square solution approximates the theoretical $\beta$ value when $n$ is large:

$$
\hat{\beta} = (\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})^{-1}\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \xrightarrow{N\;\rightarrow\;\infty} (\text{E}[XX^T])^{-1}\text{E[XY]} = \beta
$$

<details>
<summary> Remark on the limit of the least square solution </summary>
:::{.remark}
First, we observe that $\textbf{X}$ is a $N \times p$ matrix, where $N$ is the number of input. Since each row is a data sample, we can rewrite the matrix as:

$$
\textbf{X} =  \begin{bmatrix}x_1^T\\x_2^T\\\vdots\\x_N^T\end{bmatrix}
$$

We can now rewrite $\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}$ Eqn \@ref(eq:normsol) as:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \; x_2 \; ... \; x_n \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x_1^T\\x_2^T\\\vdots\\x_N^T\end{bmatrix}
                      &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_ix^T_i \\
                      &\approx N\text{E}[XX^T]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Next, we observe that $\textbf{y}$ is a $N$-vector of constants:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \; x_2 \; ... \; x_N \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}y_1\\y_2\\\vdots\\y_N\end{bmatrix}
                       &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_iy_i \\
                       &\approx N\text{E}[XY]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\hat{\beta} &= (\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X})^{-1}\textbf{X}^T\textbf{y} \\
           &\approx (N\text{E}[XX^T])^{-1}N \text{E}[XY] \\
           &\xrightarrow{N\;\rightarrow\;\infty} \frac{1}{N}(\text{E}[XX^T])^{-1}N\text{E}[XY] \\
           &= \text{E}[XX^T])^{-1}\text{E}[XY] \\
           &= \beta
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$
Thus, we have shown that the least square solution approaches the theoretical $\beta$ as $n$ gets larger.
:::
</details>

### L1 loss function

We want to show that given $L^1$ loss, the $\text{EPE}(f)$ is minimized by $f(x) = \text{median}(Y \mid X = x)$. We first have that the median for any distribution is:

$$
\begin{equation}
c^* = \inf\{t : F_X(t) \geq \tfrac{1}{2}\} = Q\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)
\end{equation}
$$

where $Q(p) := F_X^{-1}(p)$ is the quantile function.

We show that the median $c^*$ satisfies both:

$$
\begin{equation}
\text{(i)}\ P(X \leq c^*) \geq \frac{1}{2} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \text{(ii)}\ P(X \geq c^*) \geq \frac{1}{2}
\end{equation}
$$


To establish (i), take a sequence $\{t_n\}$ with $t_n \downarrow c^*$. Since each $t_n > c^* = \inf\{t : F_X(t) \geq 1/2\}$, each $t_n$ belongs to $\{t : F_X(t) \geq 1/2\}$, so $F_X(t_n) \geq 1/2$ for every $n$. By right-continuity of $F_X$, we have $F_X(t_n) \to F_X(c^*)$ as $t_n \downarrow c^*$, and hence $F_X(c^*) \geq 1/2$ and $P(X \leq c^*) \geq 1/2$.

To establish (ii), let $t < c^*$. By definition of the infimum, $t \notin \{s : F_X(s) \geq 1/2\}$, so $F_X(t) < 1/2$. Taking $t \uparrow c^*$, we obtain $F_X(c^{*-}) \leq 1/2$. Since $F_X(c^{*-}) = P(X < c^*)$, it follows that $P(X < c^*) \leq 1/2$, and therefore:

$$
\begin{equation}
P(X \geq c^*) = 1 - P(X < c^*) \geq \frac{1}{2}
\end{equation}
$$

We now show that the median minimizes expected absolute deviation. Let $X$ be a real-valued random variable with $\text{E}[|X|] < \infty$, and let $c^*$ be its median. Then for every $c \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\text{E}[|X - c|] \geq \text{E}[|X - c^*|]
\end{equation}
$$

We analyze three cases: (i) $c = c^*$, (ii) $c > c^*$, (iii) $c < c^*$.

(i) $c = c^*$, the inequality holds with equality.

(ii) Suppose that $c > c^*$, and let $\delta = c - c^* > 0$. We partition the real line into three events:

$$
\begin{equation}
A = \{X \leq c^*\}, \quad B = \{c^* < X \leq c\}, \quad C = \{X > c\}
\end{equation}
$$

We compute the pointwise difference $|X - c| - |X - c^*|$ on each event. On $A$, since $X \leq c^* < c$:

$$
\begin{equation}
|X - c| - |X - c^*| = (c - X) - (c^* - X) = c - c^* = \delta
\end{equation}
$$

On $B$, since $c^* < X \leq c$:

$$
\begin{equation}
|X - c| - |X - c^*| = (c - X) - (X - c^*) = c + c^* - 2X
\end{equation}
$$

On $C$, since $X > c > c^*$:

$$
\begin{equation}
|X - c| - |X - c^*| = (X - c) - (X - c^*) = -c + c^* = -\delta
\end{equation}
$$

Integrating over the partition:

$$
\begin{equation}
\text{E}[|X - c|] - \text{E}[|X - c^*|] = \delta P(A) - \delta P(C) + \text{E}[(c + c^* - 2X)\mathbf{1}_B]
\end{equation}
$$

We simplify the $B$-term by writing $c + c^* - 2X = 2(c - X) - \delta$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\text{E}[(c + c^* - 2X)\mathbf{1}_B] = 2\,\text{E}[(c - X)\mathbf{1}_B] - \delta P(B)
\end{equation}
$$

Substituting back and using $P(A) + P(B) + P(C) = 1$ to write $P(A) - P(C) - P(B) = 2P(A) - 1$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\text{E}[|X - c|] - \text{E}[|X - c^*|] = \delta\bigl(2P(X \leq c^*) - 1\bigr) + 2\,\text{E}[(c - X)\mathbf{1}_B]
\end{equation}
$$

The first term is non-negative since $\delta > 0$ and $P(X \leq c^*) \geq 1/2$ by (i). The second term is non-negative since on $B$ we have $X \leq c$, hence $c - X \geq 0$. Consequently $\text{E}[|X - c|] \geq \text{E}[|X - c^*|]$ for $c > c^*$.

(iii) Suppose that $c < c^*$, and let $\delta = c^* - c > 0$. We partition the real line into:

$$
\begin{equation}
A' = \{X < c\}, \quad B' = \{c \leq X < c^*\}, \quad C' = \{X \geq c^*\}
\end{equation}
$$

The same pointwise computation gives, on $A'$:

$$
\begin{equation}
|X - c| - |X - c^*| = (c - X) - (c^* - X) = c - c^* = -\delta
\end{equation}
$$

on $B'$:

$$
\begin{equation}
|X - c| - |X - c^*| = (X - c) - (c^* - X) = 2X - c - c^*
\end{equation}
$$

and on $C'$:

$$
\begin{equation}
|X - c| - |X - c^*| = (X - c) - (X - c^*) = c^* - c = \delta
\end{equation}
$$

Integrating and simplifying the $B'$-term by writing $2X - c - c^* = 2(X - c) - \delta$:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{E}[|X - c|] - \text{E}[|X - c^*|] &= -\delta P(A') + \delta P(C') + 2\,\text{E}[(X-c)\mathbf{1}_{B'}] - \delta P(B') \\
&= \delta\bigl(2P(X \geq c^*) - 1\bigr) + 2\,\text{E}[(X - c)\mathbf{1}_{B'}]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

where we used $P(C') - P(A') - P(B') = 2P(C') - 1$. The first term is non-negative by (ii), and the second is non-negative since $X \geq c$ on $B'$. Hence $\text{E}[|X - c|] \geq \text{E}[|X - c^*|]$ for $c < c^*$.

In all three cases the inequality holds.

Therefore under $L^1$ loss, the function $f^*(x) = \text{median}(Y \mid X = x)$ minimizes the expected prediction error $\text{EPE}(f) = \text{E}[|Y - f(X)|]$

Given $\text{EPE}(f) = \text{E}[|Y - f(X)|]$, we condition on $X$ and minimize pointwise:

$$
\begin{equation}
\text{E}[|Y - f(X)|] = \text{E}_X\,\text{E}_{Y|X}[|Y - c| \mid X = x], \qquad c = f(x)
\end{equation}
$$

Hence:

$$
\begin{equation}
f(x) = \operatorname*{argmin}_c\, \text{E}_{Y|X}[|Y - c| \mid X = x]
\end{equation}
$$

As we have established $\text{E}[|X - c|] \geq \text{E}[|X - c^*|]$ for every $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the constant $c^*$ is the global minimizer. Hence $f(x) = \text{median}(Y \mid X = x)$ is the solution.

### Optimal Function of Categorical Variable

Suppose that the output is a categorical variable $G$, a different loss function is needed. Let $\hat{G}$ be an estimate of values in $\mathcal{G}$, the set of all possible classes. Our loss function can be represented as a $K \times K$ matrix $\textbf{L}$, where $K = \text{card}(\mathcal{G})$. $\textbf{L}$ will be zero on the diagonal and nonnegative elsewhere, where $L(k, \ell)$ is the loss for classifying an observation belonging to $\mathcal{G}_k$ as $\mathcal{G}_\ell$, i.e.

$$
\begin{equation}
\textbf{L} = \begin{bmatrix}
              0 & L(\mathcal{G}_1, \mathcal{G}_2) & L(\mathcal{G}_1, \mathcal{G}_3) & \dots  & L(\mathcal{G}_1, \mathcal{G}_K) \\
              L(\mathcal{G}_2, \mathcal{G}_1) & 0 & L(\mathcal{G}_2, \mathcal{G}_3) & \dots  & L(\mathcal{G}_2, \mathcal{G}_K) \\
              \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
              L(\mathcal{G}_K, \mathcal{G}_1) & L(\mathcal{G}_K, \mathcal{G}_2) & L(\mathcal{G}_K, \mathcal{G}_3) & \dots  & 0
\end{bmatrix}
(\#eq:Lmatrix)
\end{equation}
$$

We use the zero-one function, where misclassifications are penalized by a single unit, such that:

$$
L(k, \ell) =
\begin{cases}
0, \text{if} \; k=\ell \\
1, \text{if} \; k \neq \ell
\end{cases}
$$

w.r.t. the joint distribution $\text{Pr}(G, X)$, we have that the $\text{EPE}$ is:

$$
\text{EPE} = \text{E}[L(G, \hat{G}(X))]
$$

We condition and write $\text{EPE}$ as:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\text{EPE} &= \text{E}_x[\text{E}_{G|X}[L(G,\hat{G}(X)|X)]] \\
           &=\text{E}_x[\sum^{K}_{k=1}L(\mathcal{G}_k, \hat{G}(X)\text{P}(\mathcal{G}|X)]
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Minimizing pointwise we get:

$$
\hat{G}(x)  =\operatorname*{argmin}_{g\in \mathcal{G}} \sum^{K}L(G=\mathcal{G}_k, \hat{G}(x) = g)\;\text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x)
$$

Simplifying the loss function:

$$
\begin{equation}
L(G = \mathcal{G}_k, \hat{G}(x) = g) =
\begin{cases}
L(g,g) = 0, \text{when}\;\mathcal{G}_k =  g\\
L(\mathcal{G}_k, g) = 1 , \text{when} \; \mathcal{G}_k \neq g
\end{cases}
\end{equation}
$$

Thus, the solution to our minimizer can be simplified into:

$$
\hat{G}(x) = \operatorname*{argmin}_{g\in\mathcal{G}} \sum^K_{k:\mathcal{G}_k \neq g} 1\cdot \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x)
$$

As $\text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x)$ must sum to 1 and that the set $\mathcal{G}$ only consists of elements that is $g$ or not $g$, we have that:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\sum^K_{k=1}  \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x) &= 1 \\
\sum_{k:\mathcal{G}_k = g}  \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x) &+ \sum_{k:\mathcal{G}_k \neq g}  \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x) = 1 \\
\text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k = g|X=x) &+ \sum_{k:\mathcal{G}_k \neq g}  \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x) = 1 \\
\sum_{k:\mathcal{G}_k \neq g}\text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x) &= 1 - \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k = g|X=x)
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Thus our minimizer is simplified into:

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\hat{G}(x) = \operatorname*{argmin}_{g\in\mathcal{G}} \sum^K_{k:\mathcal{G}_k \neq g} \text{P}(\mathcal{G}_k|X=x) &= \operatorname*{argmin}_{g\in\mathcal{G}}(1-\text{P}(g|X=x)) \\ &= \operatorname*{argmax}_{g\in\mathcal{G}} \text{P}(g|X=x)
\end{split}
(\#eq:Bclass)
\end{equation}
$$

The solution is known as a $\textit{Bayes classifier}$, and implies that the best solution is found when we classify to the most probable class, using the conditional (discrete) distribution of $P(G|X)$.

```{r bayes, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Optimal Bayes decision boundary"}
compute_mixture_density <- function(x, centers, var) {
  n_components <- nrow(centers)
  density <- 0
  for (i in 1:n_components){
    density <- density + dmvnorm(x, centers[i, ], sigma = diag(var, 2))
  }

  density / n_components
}

grid_data$prob_blue <- apply(grid_data[, c("x1", "x2")], 1, function(x) compute_mixture_density(x, d1, 1 / 5))
grid_data$prob_orange <- apply(grid_data[, c("x1", "x2")], 1, function(x) compute_mixture_density(x, d2, 1 / 5))

grid_data$predicted_class <- ifelse(grid_data$prob_orange > grid_data$prob_blue, "Orange", "Blue")
grid_data$pred_numeric <- as.numeric(grid_data$predicted_class == "Orange")

# Plot
ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = predicted_class),
             size = 0.5, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_contour(data = grid_data, aes(x = x1, y = x2, z = pred_numeric),
               breaks = 0.5, size = 0.8, color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = test_mat, aes(x = x1, y = x2, color = Y),
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8, stroke = 1, shape = 1) +
  labs(title = "Bayes Optimal Classifier") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#4A90E2", "#F5A623")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.3, linetype = "dotted"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_fixed(ratio = 0.75)
```

$k$-nearest neighbor approximates the Bayesian solution - except that conditional probability at a point is relaxed to conditional probability within a neighborhood of a point.

## Local Methods in High Dimensions

### Neighborhood in n-dimensional volume

Our intuition suggests that, given a sufficiently large training set, we could always approximate the theoretically optimal conditional expectation through $k$-nearest neighbor averaging — since we should be able to find a large neighborhood of observations close to $x$ and average over them. This intuition, however, breaks down in high dimensions.

Consider the $p$-dimensional unit hypercube. Suppose we send out a hypercubial neighborhood around a target point to capture a fraction $r$ of the observations, and since this corresponds to a fraction $r$ of the unit volume, we have that the expected edge length will be $e_p(r) = r^{\frac{1}{p}}$. As the dimensions of the dataset increase, the expected edge length increase such that the neighborhood can no longer be considered "local". Reducing $r$ does not help, since the fewer observations we average, the higher the variance becomes.

```{r volume, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Curse of dimensionality illustrated. 80% of the range of each coordinate will be needed to capture 10% of the data in a 10-dimensional hypercube."}
r <- seq(0, 0.65, length.out = 500)
dims <- c(1, 2, 3, 10)
df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(dims, function(p) {
  data.frame(r = r, distance = r^(1 / p), p = factor(paste0("p=", p)))
}))

ggplot(df, aes(x = r, y = distance, color = p)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.1, 0.3), linetype = "dashed", color = "grey0", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.65), breaks = seq(0, 0.6, by = 0.2)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.2)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    x = "Fraction of Volume",
    y = "Distance"
  )
```

### Median Distance to the Nearest Neighbor

Another consequence is that sample points are close to an edge of the sample. Consider $N$ data points uniformly distributed in a $p$-dimensional ball centered at the origin. Suppose we consider a nearest-neighbor estimate at the origin. The median distance from the origin to the closest data point is:

$$
\begin{equation}
d(p, N) = \left(1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1/N}\right)^{1/p}
(\#eq:mediandist)
\end{equation}
$$

<details>
<summary>Proof of median nearest-neighbor distance formula.</summary>

The proof proceeds in three stages: computation of the Jacobian determinant of the $p$-dimensional spherical coordinate map by induction; derivation of the volume of the $p$-ball via the reduction formula for $I_n = \int_0^\pi \sin^n\theta\,d\theta$ and a telescoping product of Gamma values; and finally, the order-statistic argument yielding the median.

::: {.lemma}
Define the $p$-dimensional spherical coordinate map $\Phi_p : (r, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{p-1}) \mapsto (x_1, \ldots, x_p)$ by

$$
\begin{equation}
x_j = r\!\left(\prod_{k=1}^{j-1}\sin\theta_k\right)\cos\theta_j, \quad j = 1,\ldots,p-1,
\qquad
x_p = r\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}\sin\theta_k
(\#eq:sphericalmap)
\end{equation}
$$

where $\theta_k \in [0,\pi]$ for $k = 1,\ldots,p-2$, $\theta_{p-1} \in [0,2\pi)$, and empty products equal one. Then the Jacobian determinant is

$$
\begin{equation}
J_p := \left|\det D\Phi_p\right| = r^{p-1}\prod_{k=1}^{p-2}\sin^{p-1-k}\theta_k
(\#eq:jacobian)
\end{equation}
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The proof is by induction on $p$. The coordinate formulas satisfy the recursion

$$
\begin{equation}
\Phi_p(r,\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_{p-1}) = \bigl(r\cos\theta_1,\;\sin\theta_1\cdot\Phi_{p-1}(r,\theta_2,\ldots,\theta_{p-1})\bigr)
(\#eq:phirecursion)
\end{equation}
$$

which is verified directly: the first coordinate is $r\cos\theta_1$, and for $j \geq 2$,

$$
x_j = r\!\left(\prod_{k=1}^{j-1}\sin\theta_k\right)\cos\theta_j = r\sin\theta_1\!\left(\prod_{k=2}^{j-1}\sin\theta_k\right)\cos\theta_j = \sin\theta_1\cdot[\Phi_{p-1}(r,\theta_2,\ldots)]_j
$$

confirming that $(x_2,\ldots,x_p) = \sin\theta_1\cdot\Phi_{p-1}(r,\theta_2,\ldots,\theta_{p-1})$.

For the base case $p = 2$, the map is $(r,\theta_1)\mapsto(r\cos\theta_1, r\sin\theta_1)$ and its Jacobian determinant is

$$
\det D\Phi_2 = \det\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta_1 & -r\sin\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & r\cos\theta_1\end{pmatrix} = r\cos^2\theta_1 + r\sin^2\theta_1 = r
$$

confirming $J_2 = r$ with the empty product equal to one.

Assume inductively that $J_{p-1} = r^{p-2}\prod_{k=1}^{p-3}\sin^{p-2-k}\theta_{k+1}$, where angles are indexed from $\theta_2$. Differentiating the recursion \@ref(eq:phirecursion), the Jacobian matrix $D\Phi_p$ takes the block form

$$
D\Phi_p = \begin{pmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & -r\sin\theta_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\[4pt]
\sin\theta_1\,\partial_r\Phi_{p-1} &
\cos\theta_1\,\Phi_{p-1} &
\sin\theta_1\,\partial_{\theta_2}\Phi_{p-1} &
\cdots &
\sin\theta_1\,\partial_{\theta_{p-1}}\Phi_{p-1}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

where $\partial_r\Phi_{p-1}$, $\Phi_{p-1}$, and each $\partial_{\theta_k}\Phi_{p-1}$ are $(p-1)$-dimensional column vectors occupying rows 2 through $p$. Expanding the determinant along the first row,

$$
\det(D\Phi_p) = \cos\theta_1\cdot M_{11} + (-r\sin\theta_1)\cdot(-1)^{1+2}\cdot M_{12} = \cos\theta_1\cdot M_{11} + r\sin\theta_1\cdot M_{12}
$$

where $M_{11}$ and $M_{12}$ are the corresponding unsigned minors.

In $M_{11}$, column 1 is $\cos\theta_1\,\Phi_{p-1}$ and columns $2$ through $p-1$ are $\sin\theta_1\,\partial_{\theta_k}\Phi_{p-1}$ for $k = 2,\ldots,p-1$. Factoring $\cos\theta_1$ from the first column and $\sin\theta_1$ from each of the remaining $p-2$ columns,

$$
M_{11} = \cos\theta_1\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\cdot\det\!\bigl[\Phi_{p-1}\mid\partial_{\theta_2}\Phi_{p-1}\mid\cdots\mid\partial_{\theta_{p-1}}\Phi_{p-1}\bigr]
$$

Since every component of $\Phi_{p-1}$ is linear in $r$, the map is homogeneous of degree one, giving $\Phi_{p-1}(r,\cdot) = r\,\partial_r\Phi_{p-1}(r,\cdot)$. Replacing the first column $\Phi_{p-1}$ by $r\,\partial_r\Phi_{p-1}$ and factoring $r$ out of that column,

$$
\det\!\bigl[\Phi_{p-1}\mid\partial_{\theta_2}\Phi_{p-1}\mid\cdots\bigr] = r\cdot\det\!\bigl[\partial_r\Phi_{p-1}\mid\partial_{\theta_2}\Phi_{p-1}\mid\cdots\bigr] = r\,J_{p-1}
$$

and hence $M_{11} = r\cos\theta_1\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\,J_{p-1}$.

In $M_{12}$, every column carries a factor of $\sin\theta_1$: column 1 is $\sin\theta_1\,\partial_r\Phi_{p-1}$ and the remaining $p-2$ columns are $\sin\theta_1\,\partial_{\theta_k}\Phi_{p-1}$. Factoring $\sin\theta_1$ from all $p-1$ columns,

$$
M_{12} = \sin^{p-1}\theta_1\cdot\det\!\bigl[\partial_r\Phi_{p-1}\mid\partial_{\theta_2}\Phi_{p-1}\mid\cdots\bigr] = \sin^{p-1}\theta_1\,J_{p-1}
$$

Substituting both minors into the expansion and collecting,

$$
\det(D\Phi_p) = r\cos^2\theta_1\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\,J_{p-1} + r\sin^p\theta_1\,J_{p-1} = r\,J_{p-1}\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\underbrace{\bigl(\cos^2\theta_1 + \sin^2\theta_1\bigr)}_{=\,1} = r\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\,J_{p-1}
$$

Applying the inductive hypothesis and re-indexing the product via $m = k+1$,

$$
J_p = r\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\cdot r^{p-2}\prod_{k=1}^{p-3}\sin^{p-2-k}\theta_{k+1} = r^{p-1}\sin^{p-2}\theta_1\prod_{m=2}^{p-2}\sin^{p-1-m}\theta_m = r^{p-1}\prod_{k=1}^{p-2}\sin^{p-1-k}\theta_k
$$

where the $k=1$ term of the final product recovers $\sin^{p-2}\theta_1$, completing the induction. $\square$
:::

---

::: {.proposition}
The volume of the $p$-dimensional ball of radius $R$ is

$$
\begin{equation}
V_p(R) = \frac{\pi^{p/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{p}{2}+1\right)}R^p
(\#eq:volball)
\end{equation}
$$
:::

:::{.proof}
By the Lemma, the volume element is $r^{p-1}\prod_{k=1}^{p-2}\sin^{p-1-k}\theta_k\,dr\,d\theta_1\cdots d\theta_{p-1}$. Since the integrand separates over independent variables,

$$
\begin{equation}
V_p(R) = \underbrace{\int_0^R r^{p-1}\,dr}_{=\,R^p/p} \cdot \underbrace{\int_0^{2\pi}d\theta_{p-1}}_{=\,2\pi} \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{p-2}\underbrace{\int_0^\pi\sin^{p-1-k}\theta_k\,d\theta_k}_{=\,I_{p-1-k}}
\end{equation}
$$

where $I_n := \int_0^\pi\sin^n\theta\,d\theta$. Re-indexing via $j = p-1-k$ (as $k$ runs from $1$ to $p-2$, $j$ runs over the same set $\{1,\ldots,p-2\}$),

$$
\begin{equation}
V_p(R) = \frac{R^p}{p}\cdot 2\pi\cdot\prod_{j=1}^{p-2}I_j
(\#eq:volprod)
\end{equation}
$$

Integrating by parts with $u = \sin^{n-1}\theta$ and $dv = \sin\theta\,d\theta$, so $du = (n-1)\sin^{n-2}\theta\cos\theta\,d\theta$ and $v = -\cos\theta$,

$$
I_n = \bigl[-\sin^{n-1}\theta\cos\theta\bigr]_0^\pi + (n-1)\int_0^\pi\sin^{n-2}\theta\cos^2\theta\,d\theta
$$

Since $\sin(0) = \sin(\pi) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes. Substituting $\cos^2\theta = 1-\sin^2\theta$ and splitting,

$$
I_n = (n-1)\int_0^\pi\sin^{n-2}\theta\,d\theta - (n-1)\int_0^\pi\sin^n\theta\,d\theta = (n-1)I_{n-2} - (n-1)I_n
$$

Adding $(n-1)I_n$ to both sides and dividing by $n$,

$$
\begin{equation}
I_n = \frac{n-1}{n}\,I_{n-2}
(\#eq:Inreduction)
\end{equation}
$$

with base cases $I_0 = \pi$ and $I_1 = 2$.

For even $n = 2m$ with $m \geq 1$, applying \@ref(eq:Inreduction) repeatedly until reaching $I_0$,

$$
\begin{equation}
I_{2m} = \frac{2m-1}{2m}\cdot\frac{2m-3}{2m-2}\cdots\frac{3}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\cdot I_0 = \frac{(2m-1)!!}{(2m)!!}\,\pi
(\#eq:Ieven)
\end{equation}
$$

For odd $n = 2m+1$ with $m \geq 0$, applying \@ref(eq:Inreduction) repeatedly until reaching $I_1$,

$$
\begin{equation}
I_{2m+1} = \frac{2m}{2m+1}\cdot\frac{2m-2}{2m-1}\cdots\frac{4}{5}\cdot\frac{2}{3}\cdot I_1 = \frac{(2m)!!}{(2m+1)!!}\cdot 2
(\#eq:Iodd)
\end{equation}
$$

Using the double factorial identities $(2m)!! = 2^m m!$ and $(2m-1)!! = 2^m\Gamma(m+\tfrac{1}{2})/\sqrt{\pi}$, we substitute into \@ref(eq:Ieven) for the even case:

$$
I_{2m} = \frac{\dfrac{2^m\,\Gamma(m+\frac{1}{2})}{\sqrt{\pi}}}{2^m m!}\,\pi = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(m+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)}{m!} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(m+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma(m+1)}
$$

Setting $n = 2m$, so $m = n/2$, gives $I_n = \sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{n+1}{2}\bigr)/\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{n}{2}+1\bigr)$.

For the odd case, the functional equation $\Gamma(m+\tfrac{3}{2}) = (m+\tfrac{1}{2})\Gamma(m+\tfrac{1}{2})$ applied recursively gives $(2m+1)!! = 2^{m+1}\Gamma(m+\tfrac{3}{2})/\sqrt{\pi}$. Substituting into \@ref(eq:Iodd),

$$
I_{2m+1} = \frac{2^m m!}{\dfrac{2^{m+1}\Gamma(m+\frac{3}{2})}{\sqrt{\pi}}}\cdot 2 = \frac{2^{m+1}m!\sqrt{\pi}}{2^{m+1}\Gamma\!\left(m+\tfrac{3}{2}\right)} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma(m+1)}{\Gamma\!\left(m+\tfrac{3}{2}\right)}
$$

Setting $n = 2m+1$, so $\Gamma(m+1) = \Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{n+1}{2}\bigr)$ and $\Gamma(m+\tfrac{3}{2}) = \Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{n}{2}+1\bigr)$, gives the same expression. Both parities therefore yield the unified form

$$
\begin{equation}
I_n = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}+1\right)}
(\#eq:Ingamma)
\end{equation}
$$

Substituting $I_n$ into the product in \@ref(eq:volprod), and writing $\sqrt{\pi} = \Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)$ and using the identity $\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)^{p-2} = \pi^{(p-2)/2}$,

$$
\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\prod_{j=1}^{p-2}I_j = \prod_{j=1}^{p-2}\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{j+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{j+2}{2}\right)}
                     = \pi^{(p-2)/2}\prod_{j=1}^{p-2}\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{j+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{j+2}{2}\right)}
\end{split}
\end{equation}
$$

Writing the product explicitly,

$$
\prod_{j=1}^{p-2}\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{j+1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{j+2}{2}\right)} = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{2}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{3}{2}\right)}\cdot\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{3}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{4}{2}\right)}\cdot\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{4}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{5}{2}\right)}\cdots\frac{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p-1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}\right)}
$$

all interior terms cancel telescopically, leaving

$$
\frac{\Gamma(1)}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}\right)} = \frac{1}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}\right)}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{equation}
\prod_{j=1}^{p-2}I_j = \frac{\pi^{(p-2)/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}\right)}
(\#eq:prodIj)
\end{equation}
$$

Substituting \@ref(eq:prodIj) into \@ref(eq:volprod),

$$
V_p(R) = \frac{R^p}{p}\cdot 2\pi\cdot\frac{\pi^{(p-2)/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}\right)} = \frac{2\pi^{p/2}R^p}{p\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}\right)}
$$

Applying $\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{p}{2}+1\bigr) = \tfrac{p}{2}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{p}{2}\bigr)$, so that $p\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{p}{2}\bigr) = 2\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{p}{2}+1\bigr)$

$$
V_p(R) = \frac{2\pi^{p/2}R^p}{2\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}+1\right)} = \frac{\pi^{p/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{p}{2}+1\right)}R^p \qquad\square
$$
:::

---

::: {.proof}
Since $X_1$ is uniformly distributed on $\mathbb{B}^p$, the probability that $X_1$ lies within distance $m$ of the origin is the ratio of volumes. By the Proposition, the normalization constants cancel:

$$
P(\|X_1\|\leq m) = \frac{V_p(m)}{V_p(1)} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi^{p/2}}{\Gamma(p/2+1)}\,m^p}{\dfrac{\pi^{p/2}}{\Gamma(p/2+1)}} = m^p
$$

Thus the CDF of $\|X_1\|$ is $F(m) = m^p$. Let $D_{(1)} = \min_{i=1}^{N}\|X_i\|$. The event $\{D_{(1)} > m\}$ is the intersection of $N$ independent events, each with probability $1-m^p$, so

$$
P(D_{(1)} > m) = \prod_{i=1}^{N}P(\|X_i\| > m) = (1-m^p)^N
$$

and therefore $P(D_{(1)}\leq m) = 1-(1-m^p)^N$. The median $d = d(p,N)$ satisfies $P(D_{(1)}\leq d) = \tfrac{1}{2}$, giving

$$
1-(1-d^p)^N = \tfrac{1}{2} \implies (1-d^p)^N = \tfrac{1}{2} \implies 1-d^p = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{1/N} \implies d(p,N) = \left(1-\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{1/N}\right)^{1/p}
$$
:::

</details>
<br>

For $p = 10$ and $N = 500$, this gives $d(10,500) \approx 0.52$, meaning the nearest neighbor is typically more than halfway to the boundary. Thus the nearest neighbor of the origin is not at all local, and this effect worsens rapidly with dimension.

[^1]: For a detailed proof of the derivation of the normal equation, see Appendix A at the end of this chapter.
[^2]: Proof for $\text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T\textbf{X}) = \text{Col}(\textbf{X}^T)$ can be accessed at this [Stack Exchange post](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/3573576/given-an-n-times-m-matrix-a-prove-that-\text{Col}ata-colat)
[^3]: Derivation of the first-order characterization of a convex function can be accessed at this [pdf file](https://www.princeton.edu/~aaa/Public/Teaching/ORF523/S16/ORF523_S16_Lec7_gh.pdf)
[^4]: If $V$ is a nontrivial linear subspace, then there exists some nonzero vector $v\in V$. As $V$ is under scalar multiplication, $cv\in V$ for any scalar $c\in\mathbb{R}$. As $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable, it follows that $V$ has uncountable many elements
[^5]: Essentially, the $p$-dim predicted surface generated by the statistical model is determined by the $p$ parameters. A simple linear regression of the form $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1$ forms a line, characterized by ($\beta_0$, $\beta_1$) where all possible values reside. On the other hand, a linear regression of two input variables $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1 + \beta_2x_2$ forms a plane, characterized by ($\beta_0$, $\beta_1$, $\beta_2$) where all possible values reside.
[^6]: Generally, the distance metric is specified by the Minkowski distance
    $d(\textbf{x}, \textbf{z}) = (\sum_{r=1}^N |x_i - z_i|^p)^{\frac{1}{p}}$
    Special cases include: $p = 1$ (Manhattan Distance), <br> $p = 2$ (Euclidean Distance), <br> $p \rightarrow \infty$ (Chebyshev Distance).
[^7]: Every point in $\mathcal{T}$ but not in $N_k$, is at least as far away from $\textbf{x}$ as the furthest point in $N_k$
